items.q: What is the difference between relief and intaglio printing?
items.correct: Relief prints from raised surfaces; intaglio prints from incised lines below the surface.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Relief is for paper, intaglio is for fabric.
items.why: Students don't grasp the inverse logic. Lino cuts AWAY non-printing areas (relief - raised inked); etching incises lines that HOLD ink below the surface (intaglio - ink pressed up into paper).

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.q: What is the central innovation of Cubism?
items.correct: Showing an object from multiple viewpoints simultaneously on a flat surface.
items.why: Students take the name literally. Cubism is named for facet-like fragmentation, but it isn't about cubes - it's about breaking the single Renaissance viewpoint into many at once.

items.q: Whose triadic sign distinguishes icon, index and symbol?
items.why: It's Peirce. “Saussure” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Whose 1964 Rhetoric of the Image analysed Panzani's pasta ad?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Barthes. “Metz” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Mitchell publish Picture Theory?
items.why: Exact answer: 1994 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
